title: The 21st-Century Steven Spielberg Rankings. Plus: ‘Office Romance’ and ‘The Furious.’
format: In the wake of Disclosure Day's opening weekend, Sean and Amanda review two new releases (the J.Lo Netflix rom-com Office Romance and the martial-arts movie The Furious), take a second pass at Spielberg's UFO film, then rank all 17 of his features from 2000 on.

films.title: Disclosure Day
films.note: Opened to $44M domestic and $93M worldwide, beating soft tracking. 59% of the audience was over 34. It's Spielberg's first real summer hit since Minority Report.

films.title: Obsession
films.note: Still drawing crowds weeks into its run as the box office keeps posting $40M openings.

films.title: Office Romance
films.note: Jennifer Lopez as the heir-CEO of a budget airline who can't act on her spark with in-house counsel Brett Goldstein (who also wrote it) because of the company's anti-fraternization rule. Sean liked it more than he expected, with a decent script and good one-liners, but was annoyed by the New Jersey tax-credit cheapness, singling out the Anthropologie-by-AI bedroom set. Loaded supporting bench: Edward James Olmos, Bradley Whitford, Tony Hale, Jodie Whittaker, and Betty Gilpin.

segments.heading: The Jennifer Lopez Hall of Fame
segments.note: a would-be episode they sketched while Office Romance played

films.title: Selena
films.note: The one Eileen asked for first. With Out of Sight, head and shoulders above the rest of the filmography.

films.title: Out of Sight
films.note: The other undeniable classic; Sean and Amanda's two picks for her best.

films.title: Anaconda
films.note: Her action breakout.

films.title: The Cell
films.note: Tarsem Singh's serial-killer thriller. The second DVD Sean ever owned, a gift from Eileen in high school.

films.title: The Wedding Planner
films.note: They staged real weddings for it, Amanda notes.

films.title: Maid in Manhattan
films.note: With Ralph Fiennes. Amanda rewatched it recently and found who it laughs at, and why, badly dated.

films.title: Enough
films.note: Michael Apted directs. Preposterous, not bad, and pure meme fuel from the poster.

films.title: Gigli
films.note: A terrible film, but a pop-cultural must.

films.title: Hustlers
films.note: The late-career high, a 20-year jump from her early run.

films.title: Shotgun Wedding
films.note: Not bad, and better than Office Romance to Sean: at least it sprang for a resort and a good end-credits cameo.

films.title: Halftime
films.note: The Super Bowl documentary. The Golden Globe crying scene lives rent-free in Sean's head.

films.title: The Greatest Love Story Never Told
films.note: The making-of documentary for her This Is Me… Now project.

films.title: The Furious
films.note: A pan-Asian martial-arts revenge movie Sean calls a possible instant classic. A mute, highly trained father (Xie Miao) hunts a child-abduction ring after his daughter is taken, teaming with another lethal fighter (Joe Taslim). Near hand-to-hand only, brutal, with inventive use of ice, and a five-way showdown that splits into a five-panel close-up of the fighters' eyes that had the crowd on its feet.

films.title: Disclosure Day
films.note: Amanda's second watch left her cold on the jammed-on Christian undertones and the device-like Eve Hewson character, but blown away by the tapes and the news-anchor sequence (Courtney Grace). They read it as a post-COVID allegory about whether anything could still unite us, note Sarah Broshar taking over from longtime editor Michael Kahn, and wonder whether Tony Kushner turned the script down.

segments.heading: 21st-Century Steven Spielberg, ranked
segments.note: all 17 features since 2000, best to worst
segments.subject: Steven Spielberg

films.title: The Fabelmans
films.note: No. 1, audaciously. His Killers of the Flower Moon: an origin story that fuses his biography with the movies, hand in hand with Disclosure Day.

films.title: Catch Me If You Can
films.note: No. 2. His most purely pleasurable film of the century: old-school Spielberg about a father and son putting on a show.

films.title: Lincoln
films.note: A five-star masterpiece to Sean, overlooked despite Day-Lewis's Best Actor.

films.title: Minority Report
films.note: A banger. Peak Cruise-Spielberg energy that still holds up as pure entertainment.

films.title: Munich
films.note: Sean's happiest rediscovery, maybe his best-ever filmmaking. The start of the Tony Kushner partnership, held back only by Eric Bana's central miscasting.

films.title: A.I. Artificial Intelligence
films.note: Sean's real No. 1 and the film he'd have put on their 25-for-25 list: the biggest world of the bunch, fusing Kubrick's sensibility with his own. Amanda has never seen it.

films.title: West Side Story
films.note: Amanda rides hard for it; Sean calls the filmmaking extraordinary but docks it as a straight remake.

films.title: War of the Worlds
films.note: Harrowing and alive, a direct response to 9/11 and the only present-day film of his recent run.

films.title: Bridge of Spies
films.note: No. 9. A banger, especially the first half; one of his best period pieces. Sean was surprised how much he liked the revisit.

films.title: Disclosure Day
films.note: Tied at 11 with The Post: twin films about revelation and an Englishman prying secrets loose. Sean gives it the edge for having more to chew on.

films.title: The Post
films.note: Tied at 11. Handsome and old-fashioned, a strong Streep performance, a little trapped in its first-wave-Trump moment.

films.title: Ready Player One
films.note: Flawed and goofy, but Sean rates the world-building and Mark Rylance's tech-mogul turn, and thinks its point about online identity was right.

films.title: The Terminal
films.note: A kind, minor movie about a displaced man in purgatory; they tie it to Bridge of Spies' interest in people othered by bureaucracy.

films.title: War Horse
films.note: Elegant, and the film that famously enraged Amanda's father. She couldn't get through the rewatch.

films.title: Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull
films.note: The one movie here Sean wishes he hadn't made, a cash-in, aliens and all.

films.title: The Adventures of Tintin
films.note: Co-directed with Peter Jackson. A curiosity nobody could quite explain the appeal of, beyond the digital-animation experiment.

films.title: The BFG
films.note: Last place. Sean, a Roald Dahl kid, calls it misconceived from the start, mostly down to how the BFG looks.

films: Close Encounters of the Third Kind
Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny
